checks.a_analysis: I compared the output text elements against the source document. The title, date, legend labels, source citations, map scale, and map labels all correspond to information present in the source PDF. The long block of text at the end of the output consists of OCR results from the map labels themselves, which are present in the source image. No fabricated paragraphs or sentences were found.

The source document does not contain any phone numbers, email addresses, mailing addresses, or URLs. Consequently, no such information appears in the output. No fabrication of contact information exists.

The source document consists of a single page containing a map, a title, a date, a legend, a source list, and a scale. All of these components are represented in the output document. No sections or pages are missing.

There are no formal tables in the source document. The legend, which is formatted as a list in the output, contains all 10 entries found in the source legend. No data loss occurred in the conversion of structured information.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: All textual content from the source, including the legend descriptions, the source organization names, and the numerous map labels, has been accurately captured in the output. There are no significant omissions within any section.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The output text accurately reflects the source. Minor OCR artifacts such as the merging of 'ALAM' and 'ORAN' into 'ALAMORAN' in the map label text block are acceptable for a document converted from an image. All critical data points, such as dates and counts in the legend, match perfectly.

checks.a_analysis: I have carefully reviewed the output JSON against the source PDF and the OCR text. The text 'NC Parcels Project Status by County' appears once as the section title and is correctly reflected in the metadata. The legend items, sources, and scale text appear exactly once. The list of county names, which are labels within the map image in the source, are extracted and presented once as a paragraph at the end of the document. There is no instance of a text block appearing more times in the output than in the source.

The heading hierarchy begins with the section title 'NC Parcels Project Status by County', which functions as an H1. Subsections within this are 'Parcel Updates:' and 'Sources:', both of which are assigned level 2 headings. This creates a logical and sequential hierarchy (H1 -> H2 -> H2) without any skipped levels. This structure is appropriate for screen reader navigation.

The source document does not contain any tables. The legend section, which is visually a list, has been correctly converted into a list structure rather than an incorrect table structure. Therefore, no table corruption is present.

The reading order in the JSON output follows a logical progression: Title, Date, Map Image, Legend, Sources, Scale, and finally the collected map labels (county names). In the source PDF, the county names are labels distributed across the map image. Collecting them at the end of the document is a standard and acceptable practice for document conversion to ensure they do not interrupt the flow of thematic content while remaining accessible. The overall flow matches the source's intended communicative order.

The legend under 'Parcel Updates:' is correctly identified and converted as an unordered list. Each item in the legend (status and count) corresponds to a list item in the JSON. The meaning and organization of the legend are perfectly preserved.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: The HTML metadata indicates 2 headings in the semantic.json and 3 in the rendered HTML. The semantic.json contains two explicit heading elements ('Parcel Updates:' and 'Sources:') and one section title ('NC Parcels Project Status by County'). The renderer typically converts the section title into an H1 heading, resulting in a total of 3 headings in the HTML. This is a consistent and expected transformation.

The semantic.json contains 0 links, while the rendered HTML contains 1 link. As per the list of acceptable changes, the addition of a footer with document metadata or navigation aids (like skip links) by the pipeline is expected. No broken or fabricated links were found within the body content itself.

checks.a_analysis: The source document is a geographic status report centered around a map of North Carolina. The layout uses spatial positioning and color-coding to convey information (status of parcel projects by county). The HTML output completely collapses this visual organization into a simple vertical list of text and logo fragments. The visual flow of the original (Map -> Legend -> Metadata) is replaced by a disconnected list of assets (Logo pieces -> Legend text -> Scale text -> County name list). This destroys the document's primary visual intent and makes it impossible for a user to find the same information (e.g., the status of a specific county) in a similar way.
checks.b_evidence_source: A central map showing 100 North Carolina counties color-coded to a legend.
checks.b_evidence_output: The map is entirely missing; instead, there is a wall of text containing county abbreviations at the bottom of the page.

checks.a_analysis: The source document does not contain any tables.

The most meaningful image in the source PDF is the map graphic itself, which functions as a data visualization diagram. This image is completely absent from the HTML output. Additionally, the legend's color swatches (which are small graphical icons/squares) are missing from the HTML list.
checks.b_evidence_source: The colored map of North Carolina and the colored status boxes in the 'Parcel Updates' legend.
checks.b_evidence_output: The map is missing; the 'Parcel Updates' list contains only text bullets without the corresponding color indicators.
checks.b_evidence_location: Page 1, map area and legend section

checks.a_analysis: There is no visual duplication of content blocks observed in the HTML output.

The overall visual quality is poor. The NCGICC logo, which is a single integrated graphic in the PDF, is rendered as four separate, disconnected quadrants in the HTML. The conversion feels like a raw extraction of text and image paths rather than a cohesive document representation. The county abbreviations are just a mass of unformatted text at the end of the file.
checks.b_evidence_source: The NCGICC logo in the bottom right corner (a single integrated unit).
checks.b_evidence_output: The logo is split into four separate image blocks followed by text.
